title: Dog Penis Stuck Out and Swollen (Paraphimosis): Emergency Guide
meta_desc: If your dog's penis is stuck out and swollen, it is a medical emergency called paraphimosis. Seek immediate veterinary care to prevent tissue damage.
content_html:
TL;DR: Paraphimosis is a critical medical emergency where a dog's penis cannot retract into the sheath, potentially leading to tissue death and amputation. Contact an emergency veterinarian immediately and provide a photo to help the clinic prioritize your pet’s care.

What is paraphimosis and why is my dog's penis stuck outside the sheath?

When a dog's penis remains outside the prepuce (sheath) and cannot be retracted, it is a condition called paraphimosis. This usually happens when the sheath's opening is too narrow or when hair becomes entangled around the base. Because the tissue is exposed to the air, it quickly loses moisture, becomes irritated, and begins to swell, which makes it even harder to retract.

Is my dog's inability to retract its penis a medical emergency?

  • This is considered a high-urgency medical emergency that requires immediate veterinary intervention.
  • As swelling increases, the sheath acts like a tourniquet, restricting blood flow to the area.
  • If left untreated, the tissue can become dark, cold, and necrotic (die).
  • The condition leads to severe pain and may eventually require surgical intervention or amputation.
  • You should contact an emergency veterinarian immediately.

How does sending a photo help the veterinary team triage my dog?

  • Providing a clear photo allows a triage professional to assess the color and condition of the tissue immediately.
  • Tissue that appears purple, blue, or black indicates a lack of oxygenated blood, helping the clinic prioritize your dog as a critical case.
  • Visual confirmation helps the staff prepare necessary lubricants, sedatives, or surgical tools in advance of your arrival.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Paraphimosis, where the penis is stuck outside the prepuce, warrants immediate veterinary intervention because the exposed penis rapidly becomes edematous due to compromised venous drainage; continued exposure leads to mucosal dryness, pain, and self-trauma. Initial treatment involves gentle cleansing, liberal lubrication, and manual replacement of the penis into the prepuce. In difficult cases, hypertonic solutions or cold compresses with gentle digital pressure may be useful to reduce edema. A temporary purse-string suture can maintain penile position. If these efforts are unsuccessful or the condition is of longer duration, sedation or general anesthesia may be required to incise the preputial skin to relieve venous obstruction and allow thorough examination. If the urethra is damaged, a temporary closed-system indwelling urinary catheter may be needed to prevent stricture formation. In severe cases of necrosis or gangrene, amputation of the penis and prepuce with urethrostomy may be necessary.

Chapter: Reproductive

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1402)

faqs.question: Can I try to push it back in myself?
faqs.answer: You should not attempt to force the tissue back yourself, as you may cause further trauma or pain. You can apply a water-based lubricant to keep the tissue moist while traveling to the vet, but avoid using any oils or creams not approved by a professional.

faqs.question: What causes a dog's penis to get stuck out?
faqs.answer: Common causes include hair around the sheath getting tangled, trauma during mating, a naturally narrow sheath opening, or neurological issues that prevent normal muscle retraction.

faqs.question: How will the veterinarian treat this?
faqs.answer: The vet will likely use heavy lubrication, sedation to relax the muscles, and sometimes cold compresses to reduce swelling before gently guiding the tissue back. In some cases, a small incision in the sheath is necessary.
